Expectations for businesses and markets were broadly positive as 2025 began. Hopes for a pro-business, lighter regulatory environment lifted global markets in January. However, delays and uncertainties regarding U.S. policies tempered markets in February and the U.S. pulled back sharply in March (to say nothing about the early part of April).

  • In the first quarter, broad-based weakness of U.S. equities (-5% return for the Russell 3000 Index) lagged overseas markets (7% for the MSCI EAFE Index and 3% for the MSCI Emerging Markets Index).
  • Even before the market’s tariff tantrum in April, there were inflationary pressures. Global PMIs showed moderating New Order rates and higher Prices Paid/Output for manufacturing and service activities.
  • While value indices outperformed growth, both styles had positive returns among global risk factors. It was the negative showing from Beta that overwhelmed everything else.

As our investment team meets with companies, reviews recent earnings reports, and surveys the global landscape, they note several investment dynamics that inform our positioning:

In Europe:

  • Inflation: Relatively low unemployment and ongoing de-coupling efforts (shifting away from China, Russia, and the U.S.) may contribute to inflationary pressures, which could restrict the ECB's ability to cut rates despite the region’s long term weak economic growth.
  • Technology: Ongoing delays in large projects continue to limit visibility. We anticipate a more constructive second half of 2025 for the sector.
  • Industrials: While there is continued strength in long-cycle end markets such as electrification, aerospace and defense, and infrastructure, the broader Industrials sector is still awaiting a significant cyclical recovery.

In Japan:

  • Interest Rate: Banks have performed well due to increased expectations of Bank of Japan (BOJ) rate hikes. While we anticipate further rate increases, we believe the pace will be slower than market expectations.
  • Tourism & Consumer: While export-oriented industries face headwinds from tariff uncertainties, Japan's robust tourism sector is providing significant support to retailers, restaurants, and hoteliers.
  • Electrical grid industry: Following significant investor focus on the electrical grid ecosystem (utilities, components, installation) in early 2024, although we agreed with the underlying interest, we initially viewed the strong share price increases as unwarranted in relation to the economic reality. However, since then, share prices have moderated to more attractive levels.
  • Real Estate: Despite our earlier expectation that the new NISA accounts would drive retail investor appetite for the sector's strong dividend yields, flows predominantly went into U.S. equities. With the Magnificent 7 losing momentum, the real estate sector is beginning to attract renewed attention.

The International Small Cap Portfolio outperformed the MSCI EAFE Small Cap benchmark in the first quarter of 2025, driven by strong stock selection. This outperformance was supported by broad-based strength across most regions, particularly within the Consumer Discretionary and Industrials sectors.

Regional Performance: Europe

Europe was the best performing region in the portfolio with positive contribution from Germany and Sweden and relative weakness in the United Kingdom.

A growing awareness in Europe points towards the need to decouple from foreign influences across various sectors. Following past discussions on energy independence and more recently on defense independence, concerns about tariffs are now highlighting the need for trade decoupling. Looking ahead, we anticipate a medium-term objective of greater European technology independence, viewing infrastructure like the cloud as strategic assets. As the quarter closes, the urgency to accelerate this decoupling trend in Europe appears to be growing significantly.

Case in point is the portfolio’s top contributor, RENK Group (OTCPK:RNKGF), with its 150% surge. This German company is a global leader in mission-critical drive technology for land and naval defense applications, with civilian products used in marine, industrial, and energy transition sectors. The company reported a Marine order intake of almost €50M, and the Vehicle Mobility Solutions (VMS) segment secured three major orders worth over €400M. During the quarter, RENK Group was promoted to the MDAX index of the Frankfurt Stock Exchange from the SDAX segment, which further signified its growing market presence. Swedish defense manufacturer Saab (OTCPK:SAABF) also benefited from increased military spending and Europe's shift away from U.S. reliance, with its share price rising 85% this quarter. We trimmed our positions in both Saab and RENK Group due to their strong price performance. One of the three leading stainless-steel producers in the Western Hemisphere, Acerinox (OTCPK:ACRXF) saw its shares rise by 22%. The worst appears to be over for its European business, and destocking in the U.S. market seems complete. The company benefits from the prospect of increased steel protectionism in the U.S. (representing approximately 80% of its global stainless profits) and the EU’s renewed focus on defense and infrastructure.

The United Kingdom housed some of the portfolio’s largest contributors and detractors. U.K. wealth manager St. James’s Place (OTCPK:STJPF) continued on its path to recovery. The company reported solid inflows for the fourth quarter, and management confirmed its investment outlook. We trimmed our position as the stock gained 16% during the quarter. We reduced our position in food Ingredients company Tate & Lyle (OTCQX:TATYF), which declined -16% during the quarter. Management lowered the earnings estimates as they were still experiencing pricing pressure and not yet seeing evidence of volume recovery. Software solution provider Craneware (OTCPK:CRWRF) develops software applications that enable hospitals to capture more revenues, improve pricing, and save costs. Despite returning to double-digit growth in revenue and adjusted EBITDA, shares of Craneware lost -13%.

In other parts of the European markets, during some intra-quarter weakness we added to our position in Fortnox (OTCPK:FNOXF), the Swedish provider of IT applications and solutions for small and medium sized companies had posted a strong fourth quarter. At the end of the quarter, a private consortium agreed to acquire Fortnox, and its shares soared 33%.

Regional Performance: Japan

Our Japanese holdings delivered performance in line with the benchmark's Japan return. This balanced mix of leading businesses across various sectors of the Japanese economy makes our portfolio less sensitive, though not immune to macroeconomic factors such as FX, interest rates, and tariffs.

The expectation of rising BOJ interest rates drove strong performance in banks this quarter, highlighted by Rakuten Bank's (OTCPK:RKUNF) 53% gain. However, while we expect the BOJ to continue raising rates, we believe the pace will be slower than market expectations. We have therefore pared our positions in Rakuten Bank and are currently slightly underweight Japanese banks. In contrast, Integral, Japan's only listed independent private equity firm, saw its shares drop -29% as the market anticipated a significant cost increase from rising rates. While the actual cost impact on Integral wasn't as severe as the market projected, we still decreased our position because the rising interest rates did lead to increased costs for the firm.

With tariff worries weighing on export related sectors, the portfolio was relatively shielded as the main export exposure is inbound tourism. Food & Life Companies (OTCPK:SGLOF), Japan’s leading quick-service sushi chain, was a beneficiary of the strong growth in inbound traffic. The company announced first quarter results that surpassed both guidance and consensus estimates by a wide margin and drove its share price 40% higher.

Water recycling services provider Organo (OTCPK:ORGJF) reported third-quarter results in line with expectations and increased its operating profit guidance. However, the company lowered its order guidance due to delays in large Japanese projects and a downturn in legacy semiconductor and memory markets in China. This was partially offset by an upward revision of its order forecast in Taiwan, driven by rising demand from advanced semiconductor manufacturing. Consequently, Organo's share price declined by -18%.

We exited our position in Rohto Pharmaceutical (OTCPK:RPHCF), Japan’s leading manufacturer of over the counter (OTC) health care products, which lost -17% while held in the quarter. Our initial investment in the first quarter of 2023 was based on the premise of strong management, strong brands and products, and a robust pipeline. While the investment showed early promise, our thesis deteriorated due to poor management communication with investors, a stagnation in innovation despite strong legacy brands, and the continued lack of progress in their clinical pipeline. Additionally, strategic missteps, such as venturing outside their core business to acquire a Singapore-based traditional Chinese medicine company, further weakened our conviction. While we believe in allowing companies time to prove themselves, this experience has reinforced our learning that early warning signs warrant a more cautious approach to building positions or a quicker reduction when issues arise.

Regional Performance: Asia Pacific ex Japan and Emerging Markets

Our underweight position in Asia Pacific ex Japan added to overall portfolio performance. Within the region, Johns Lyng Group (OTCPK:JLGRF), Australia’s leading integrated building services provider reported disappointing results that fell short of their own guidance, despite recent positive statements from management. Furthermore, their U.S. business showed no positive progress. Having lost confidence in management, we exited our position, with a -28% share price decline during the holding period this quarter.

Our Emerging Markets holdings delivered mixed results. The unwinding of the global AI trade negatively impacted Accton Technology, a leading Taiwan-based manufacturer of white-box network devices and a top contributor in the fourth quarter of last year. Despite reporting better-than-expected margins and continued strong growth in ASIC, Accton's share price declined by -27% during the first quarter of 2025. We continue to increase our position in Piraeus Financial Holdings S.A. (OTCPK:BPIRF), the parent company of Piraeus Bank S.A, a leading bank in Greece. The bank offers a comprehensive range of financial services to individuals and businesses. We are attracted to Piraeus's almost entirely domestic customer base, its best-in-class operations and product mix (a leader in fee/commission-generating products), and its compelling valuation relative to its peers. Share price of Piraeus leaped 37% during the quarter.

Conclusion

To note that the global trade outlook and its ramifications across economies are “fluid” (the operative term for many current evaluations), seems an understatement. We see companies girding for greater turbulence before the waters calm. Hence when they incorporated the weakness from the first quarter and the “shock and awe” inflicted in early April, the

U.S. Federal Reserve and the OECD lowered their projections for U.S. and global GDP growth. All of this has created significant price swings recently. In a few instances, we may find stocks that have so dramatically oversold that they warrant us adding to those positions. At the same time, we recognize the potential for additional rapid changes in government policies, so we are cautious about overreacting. As long-term investors, our portfolios have historically been more resilient to these types of market upheavals, and that investment approach has not changed. As always, we are available for any questions you might have.

data.title: Argenx Q1 Earnings: Less Than Perfect Report Gets Punished
data.description:
argenx overview

Shares of argenx (NASDAQ:ARGX) declined yesterday after the company missed revenue estimates for the first time since the launch of Vyvgart 13 quarters ago. Now, there are some inconsistencies in reporting–Seeking Alpha reported a $23.8 million revenue miss, and this article states that Vyvgart beat FactSet's consensus by $3 million. Either way, even the fact that argenx missed someone’s expectations was a surprise.

I was not among those who were not surprised, as these results are not only supportive of my $3.6 billion net sales estimate for the full year that I shared in my February article, but I now expect Vyvgart will exceed that estimate by at least $100 million.

I did remove argenx from our Growth Stock Forum model portfolio last month as part of its reorganization, but did so reluctantly, and I see nothing wrong in the earnings report and heard nothing concerning or surprising on the earnings call. I am maintaining my bullish view on the stock.

Vyvgart delivers strong year-over-year and good sequential net sales growth

Global net sales of Vyvgart increased 7% sequentially and 98% year-over-year to $790 million in the first quarter. As mentioned, this was either somewhat better than the Street consensus, according to FactSet, and $23.8 million worse according to Seeking Alpha.

U.S. net sales grew 5% sequentially to $681 million, and all other territories saw double-digit sequential growth.

I see no reason for concern considering the typical seasonality that most pharmaceutical products see in the United States, but I do understand that some investors are disappointed after a big sequential jump in the fourth quarter, driven by the strong launch for the treatment of chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP). And the usual seasonal factor is the reverification of benefits for patients, and this first quarter saw another one – the Medicare redesign that took effect at the start of the year and accelerated Vyvgart’s channel mix to part D, where the net price is lower.

The underlying demand for Vyvgart remained strong in the first quarter, and I expect to see the typical strong sequential net sales jump in the second quarter.

Vyvgart Hytrulo, the subcutaneously administered version, was the key growth driver, and last month’s FDA approval of the pre-filled syringe (PFS) version that also allows at-home administration should further improve Vyvgart’s growth trajectory.

Analysts on the earnings call appeared concerned about the pricing impact of the availability of the PFS version, as it goes through Medicare part D, which carries a lower net price than the healthcare provider-administered intravenous (IV) Vyvgart and the “non-PFS” Hytrulo version that both go through Medicare part B.

I consider these concerns unfounded, as I think that the significantly improved convenience of at-home administration will lead to both higher patient demand and improved persistence on the drug. These factors should more than make up for the lower net price of Vyvgart Hytrulo PFS. Management also maintained the net pricing guidance of $225,000 per patient per year for the gMG indication and $450,000 per patient per year for the CIDP indication, but they expect the situation to evolve over time and that they will provide timely updates.

Vyvgart’s growth in ex-U.S. international markets remains strong, but the revenue base is still far below that of the U.S., with only $109 million in net sales in the first quarter. This is not surprising as it takes longer to secure reimbursement in each country, and the sales ramp is generally slower, but the situation should improve in the following quarters with the approval and launch of the treatment of CIDP, as well as the launch of the PFS version of Vyvgart.

In my February article, I estimated that this year’s global net sales of Vyvgart will exceed $3.6 billion, versus the $3.31 billion analyst consensus at the time. I now expect more than $3.7 billion in global net sales and still see room for outperformance, driven by better-than-expected acceptance of Vyvgart Hytrulo PFS and by the approvals and launches of the CIDP indication in ex-U.S. territories.

I should note that discrepancies in growth expectations remain – Seeking Alpha’s consensus is now $3.56 billion, down from $3.77 billion before the Q1 report came out, and Yahoo Finance shows a $3.21 billion consensus. Either way, market expectations now seem more properly aligned and make a beat-and-raise more likely in the following quarters.

Tariffs and Most Favored Nation should not hurt ARGX in the near or long term

Management took time on the earnings call to address some of the concerns that surfaced in the last few weeks.

The first was tariffs. Pharma products are temporarily spared from tariffs, but President Trump promised they will get their own in due time. Argenx believes it will not be affected by tariffs on pharmaceutical products, as Vyvgart is manufactured in each region for that region.

The second and more recent concern is the Trump administration floating the Most Favored Nation (MFN) drug pricing reform, again. This is the resurrection of the old attempt by the first Trump administration and seeks to tie the prices of U.S. products covered by the government to international prices of these drugs.

Here too, argenx remains well positioned as it sought to price Vyvgart in a relatively narrow range across the globe. That range was not defined by the management, but I have no concerns over Vyvgart or argenx’s future products being affected by MFN, assuming it becomes reality, which is still far from certain.

Risks

In the near-term, the main risks are the broader market, and more specifically, sector sentiment. We have seen in the last few weeks that broader market concerns and tariff fallout can negatively affect the share prices of most stocks, and argenx was not insulated.

The other risk is execution. I continue to see the company’s execution as very strong, but the market apparently disagreed this week.

Long-term risks are the same as outlined in previous articles—primarily, significantly increased competition. There will be more drugs in the anti-FcRn class. UCB’s (OTCPK:UCBJY) Rystiggo is already available in global markets, and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) received FDA approval for Imaavy (nipocalimab) for the treatment of gMG in late April.

Argenx investors have also largely brushed off the positive data from the third competitor in gMG and CIDP trials – Immunovant’s (IMVT) batoclimab. As I stated previously, these results served as clinical validation for Immunovant and have positive read-through to its next-generation anti-FcRn candidate IMVT-1402, which does not carry the safety baggage of batoclimab, and these results were in line with the expectations I outlined in my February article on argenx.

More competition outside of the class is on the way as well, and Amgen’s (AMGN) CD19 antibody Uplizna should reach the market in a few quarters.

Conclusion

The market reaction does not show it, but argenx’s 2025 is off to a strong start, with strong sequential demand for Vyvgart that was partly offset by seasonal factors. The launch of Vyvgart Hytrulo PFS in the U.S. last month and the expected launches of this PFS version in ex-U.S. markets, as well as the launch of the CIDP indication in ex-U.S. markets, should result in continued strong growth throughout 2025 and in the following years.

Important pipeline events were lacking in the last few quarters, but we should not have to wait long for new catalysts. Argenx expects six phase 3 readouts, six phase 2 readouts, and four new molecules in the clinic by the end of 2026.

I still expect the investment thesis to heavily rely on Vyvgart (efgartigimod), but we should also see major de-risking for the second pipeline candidate empasiprubart in the mentioned period, and initial de-risking of other early-stage candidates.

Argenx is in a very strong financial position to execute with $3.6 billion in cash and increasing operating cash flow.

data.title: Why The U.K.-U.S. Trade Deal Won't Herald A Wider Tariff Climbdown
data.description:
The UK-US deal offers Britain much needed relief from sectoral tariffs

President Trump has revealed that the UK is the first country to receive carve-outs from some – though crucially not all – of the tariffs he has introduced over recent months.

At face value, the UK seems to have done quite well – at least relative to the reports circulating in recent days. According to the UK's press release, tariffs on British steel and aluminium go to zero, with no talk of quotas. And although UK car exports are subject to a quota of 100,000 vehicles, this essentially means that UK car exports, or the vast majority of what was exported in 2024, will see 10% tariffs rather than 27.5% (2.5%, plus Trump's additional 25% rate). Cars represent roughly 10% of UK exports to the US.

As far as we can see, the concessions offered by the UK aren’t considerable. Britain reportedly won't make changes to its Digital Services Tax, which applies to the big tech firms (though it is a relatively small revenue-raiser). And importantly, the UK has said it isn’t changing its food standards. Remember, it’s that – not tariffs – which is the more significant barrier to entry for certain US agricultural products, beef and chicken being the most notorious.

Crucially, that means the US deal won’t jeopardise British attempts to build closer trade ties with the European Union. The UK hopes to conclude a veterinary deal with the EU, which would remove the most cumbersome checks at the border and would see Britain formally aligning on food standards. This should be relatively easy, on paper, given the UK hasn’t meaningfully diverged since Brexit – on food, or on goods more generally.

Securing closer access is a key priority for the UK government, as it tries to rebuild the public finances. Both sides meet for a summit on 19 May, though this is centred more on defence than trade.

UK goods exports to the US (2023, GBP bn)

The deal shows investors that the US administration can be flexible on tariffs

That all said, details about the UK-US deal are scarce. And it is a reminder that for all the hype, this represents a relatively narrowly-focussed deal. The impact on the UK economy of these changes will be fairly negligible, given the direct hit from Trump’s tariffs wasn’t huge in the first place.

For the US, though, the deal enables the President to offer the sense of flexibility that financial markets are craving. The reaction – higher equity prices, a stronger dollar, higher Treasury yields – shows that it has had the desired effect.

It may also indirectly help the US add pressure on China. Comments from Commerce Secretary Lutnick in the Oval Office hinted that the administration, as part of the deal, will put pressure on the UK to go further on anti-dumping measures against Chinese metals. We know, separately, that raising barriers to Chinese-made products entering Canada and Mexico has been a factor in recent trade talks there. Though there’s little detail on how this will work in the UK’s case, it will almost certainly form a key US ask in negotiations with other countries.

Will this herald a wider climbdown on tariffs? We're not convinced

Still, we’re sceptical that the UK's deal will herald a much broader climbdown from the US administration. A UK deal was relatively low-hanging fruit, given the US has a trade surplus in goods with Britain (even if, ironically, UK trade statistics say the opposite). Lutnick referred to the trade relationship between both countries as 'balanced.'

If you look at cars, most of what the UK exports across the Atlantic are luxury models, which represent less of a threat to the big US domestic producers. Other countries, whose manufacturers are more direct competitors, may find it harder to negotiate carve-outs from Trump’s auto tariffs – or where deals are done, the import quotas may be less generous than the one agreed with the UK.

Remember too that even the UK hasn’t succeeded in negotiating away the 10% baseline tariff. Our base case is that this remains in place, for all countries, throughout President Trump’s term. His comments in the Oval Office today, where it said 10% was a "low number," appeared to reinforce that.

The average tariff rate on US imports has surged from 2% to 23%

Stepping back, the average tariff now charged by the US on its global imports is now around 23%, up from 2% before President Trump’s inauguration. We estimate that Trump’s auto, steel and aluminium tariffs across all countries make up less than four percentage-points of that. And were the President to introduce tariffs on pharmaceuticals, semi-conductions, timber and copper, as he has previously threatened, and as the ongoing Section 232 investigations suggest, that average would rise by a further two percentage-points.

Conversely, China’s 145% tariffs make up more than 15 percentage points of that average tariff rate, even after the recent electronics exemptions. In other words, a de-escalation with China is realistically the only thing that can meaningfully move the dial on the tariff hit. Our China economist discusses the prospect of US-China talks in our latest ING Monthly.

data.title: NRG Energy: For Those Who Know About Long-Term Investments
data.description:
Thesis

NRG Energy (NRG) is trading at all-time highs, showing strong buying interest all these years.

Moreover, the multi-year upward trend is likely to continue, as the recent financial figures confirm the company's financial health, which also supports its moderate but steadily growing dividend of $1.76 (1.5% yield).

Technically, the stock has managed to remain above the resistance level of $115, giving a new buy signal with an initial target of $135 (+15%), despite the market pressures of the market caused by tariff policies.

The stock targets long-term investors who are looking for reliable, high-quality investment opportunities in a sector with strong growth potential.

Stock Overview

NRG's stock has gained 256% the last five years. Including its consistent and steadily increasing dividends (which we will examine below), the total return reaches +318% which is three times higher than the corresponding return of the S&P 500.

This return is impressive and comes with two additional elements that add to its value. First, the stock and the sector as a whole has strong potential for further upside, assuming that energy demand continues to grow and second that NRG maintains its highly attractive financial profile.

In this sense, it has been and is likely to remain, one of the top picks in the sector as clearly reflected on the New York Stock Exchange. When compared to other companies in the industry, including much larger-cap Electric Utilities, NRG stands out for its outperformance, ranking second only to the $78 billion rocket named Constellation Energy (CEG), albeit with significantly higher volatility.

The others include NextEra (NEE) with a market cap of $136B, Southern Company (SO) at $100B, Duke Energy (DUK) at $94B, and American Electric Power (AEP) at $57B.

It is also worth noting that NRG has significantly outperformed the broader Utilities sector. Over the past five years, the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLU) has delivered a total return of +68%, while NRG has returned nearly five times as much to its shareholders.

Incidentally, the long-term chart of the sector clearly shows why it remains one of the most interesting investment destinations, with no signs that this strong and steady upward trend is likely to be reversed.

Technically, the stock gave a buy signal during the recent market volatility while April's sell-off brought the support level to $85. After nine days of gains in a row, the stock hit a new all-time high with a breakout gap. The old resistance at $110 has now turned into support. The first price target is $135.

Positive outlook

NRG Energy is a leading energy company in the United States (Texas) and is active in the production and sale of electricity, as well as in home services, serving more than 7 million customers in 24 U.S. states and 8 Canadian provinces.

The Company continues to strengthen its position in the U.S. energy sector, taking advantage of the growing needs for energy mainly due to the rapid development of data centers and artificial intelligence technologies.

At the same time, it is trying to find ways to cut emissions and shift to cleaner fuels like hydrogen in order to make its operations more sustainable. The Company continues to expand through three pillars:

First, the company announced that it will build four natural gas power plants of total capacity of 5.4 GW (in partnership with GE Vernova and Kiewit). These projects aim to meet the growing energy needs of data centers in Texas and other areas. The first plant is expected to start its operation in 2029.

Second, NRG will buy, at a cost of $560 million, six natural gas power plants from Rockland Capital, which means the addition of 738 MW to its portfolio in Texas.

Third, the company invested $2.5 million in Equilibrium Energy in order to improve energy efficiency by focusing on artificial intelligence. This is part of a broader $50 million innovation fund for the sector of energy.

NRG had a 7.45% share of the U.S. power generation market, coming third after Duke Energy (8.04%) and The Southern Company (7.99%). We must also recall that it purchased Shell's retail customers in Texas, making its position stronger (through brands like Reliant, Discount Power, and Cirro Energy).

Solid financials and dividend growth

The company continues to improve its main financial results. The last five years, it has achieved an average increase of 10% in adjusted EPS, with a forecast of 7.7% EPS CAGR through 2027. It has also kept its net debt at 2.5x-2. 75x adjusted EBITDA, with recourse debt at $10.892 billion (S&P credit rating BB, positive outlook).

Revenue remains stable with only minor fluctuations and the prospects of growth, as I mentioned earlier, are supported by healthy cash flows. FCF before growth is estimated at $2.06 billion by the end of 2024 ($10.36 per share, +12% YoY). According to the expectations, the Company's future looks more than optimistic.

In 2024, adjusted EPS stood at $6.83 (+$2.11 or 45%) vs. $4.72 in 2023. Of that increase, $1.46 came from strong business performance and $0.65 came from the company's share buyback in 2024. Nearly 11 million shares were repurchased at an average price of $87.57, which was about 5% of the total shares at the start of the year. As for the dividend, it looks sustainable based on the above and is steadily increasing.

For now, the company pays a regular quarterly dividend of $0.44 (vs. $0.4075 in 2024, $0.3775 in 2023, and $0.35 in 2022), which gives a yield of about 1.5%.

Risks

Even though NRG seems very strong and has already shown in tough times that its shareholder base is solid, it still operates in a sector where there is a lot of competition for data center projects and timing is an important factor.

There is also always the risk of changing rules and market conditions in Texas, especially with supply and demand issues and price changes.

Still, the Company has given strong guidance for 2025 and aims for 10% annual EPS growth through 2029, putting it in a good position to benefit from rising energy demand and a changing market.

Lastly, it is worth noting that the stock still looks "cheap" compared to peers, with a forward P/E a bit above 16x (even though it's trading at all-time highs).

Conclusion

The analysis of NRG reveals that this investment option demonstrates respectable qualities.

The company fundamentals, together with stock behavior, make NRG an attractive investment for defensive investors who seek to include a qualitative stock in their portfolio.

The stock price has broken through $115 and this development increases the potential for additional price growth because the stock continues to establish new historical highs.

The increasing dividend yield makes NRG more appealing and the stock receives a "BUY" recommendation.

data.title: CEVA: Fairly Priced But Well Positioned In Secular Edge AI And Connectivity Markets
data.description:
Investment Thesis

CEVA, Inc. (NASDAQ:CEVA) is a pure-play semiconductor IP licensor whose portfolio aligns with secular growth trends in connectivity and on-device AI. I rate CEVA a buy purely based on its lined-up growth prospects. While most other factors, such as profitability, momentum, or valuation, might not agree with my rating, SA growth grade agrees with my rating. The company is relatively small with solid and promising products. The company's "connect, sense, and infer" strategy focuses on enabling edge AI, wireless connectivity, IoT, and 5G solutions for smart devices. The company has a broad product suite, including Bluetooth, WiFi, and UWB IP. The NeuPro series of AI NPUs and RealSpace spatial audio software are among the fast-growing end markets.

It is important to mention that management emphasizes a multi-year horizon. This means that innovations licensed today typically generate royalties 3–5 years later. Over that long cycle, CEVA's secular exposures (such as WiFi, edge AI inference, and 5G modem adoption) should drive sustained royalty streams and licensing revenue.

Business Overview

CEVA operates a licensing business model. This means it collects upfront license fees and ongoing royalties from semiconductor customers. In 1Q 2025 earnings release, the company's licensing revenues were at $15 Mn, which is a 32% increase y/y. On the other side, royalties stood at $9.2Mn. So, for sure, royalties play a significant role in the company's revenue streams. This is a strategy that CEVA just adopted, and this shift reflects CEVA's strategy of securing new IP licenses with high engineering value. As a result of this strategy, gross margins remain very high, averaging at 86% due to the low variable cost of IP sales.

CEVA's product portfolio spans several key IP categories, such as edge AI NPUs, wireless connectivity, and audio and sensor services. However, I wanted to put much emphasis on edge AI as the market is expected to reach $143 Bn by 2034.

Let's have a look at what CEVA has done with its CEVA-NeuPro family. First, the family delivers on-device neural processing. It is a high-performance, low-power design for demanding vision and ADAS tasks. It supports advanced AI models and parallel processing of multiple video streams. The NeuPro-Nano targets ultra-low power "always on" AI for TWS headsets, wearables, sensors, and small IoT devices. In short, CEVA's edge AI NPUs are the company's key growth engine beyond wireless IP. This is because they give chip-makers a ready-made, low-power accelerator that can run modern models, process multiple video streams, and scale from earbuds to cars.

The Catalysts

CEVA's long-term potential is underpinned by several secular tailwinds. As we have said previously, edge AI is one of them. The shift from cloud to edge AI is transformative. By 2025, businesses are increasingly deploying local AI to achieve low latency and bandwidth savings. CEVA's NeuPro has been built and designed for this era. According to a report by McKinsey, on-device AI is growing rapidly. ABI Research forecasts that 40% of tinyML workloads on dedicated AI hardware by 2030. And this is why CEVA's end-to-end stack positions it well against this wave. I expect a proliferation of CEVA-licensed AI designs in cameras, sensors, AR, and smart IoT, driving licensing income now and royalties later.

WiFi 6/7 and multiprotocol IoT are also key growth catalysts for CEVA. The company's WiFi IP supports the latest standards. In the just-released earnings, CEVA reported a 12% y/y rise in WiFi chipset shipments and a 183% growth in WiFi royalties. This was driven by a mix shift towards high-ASP Wi-Fi6 modules.

CEVA is also broadening its Tier 1 customer base. This move is meant to reduce concentration risk. In 1Q 2025, the company signed 11 deals with 2 as first-time customers, across connectivity, AI, and audio use cases. I believe diversification means that delays in one customer or product line will have a limited impact on overall revenues.

The bottom line is that these catalysts align with CEVA's roadmap. The secular nature of these trends supports a long-term view. And we can already see that in the 4Q 2024 earnings results, where licensing revenue was up 21% y/y and royalties were up 9% y/y. This increase was a result of the record device shipments.

Valuation

CEVA is a relatively small company. A market cap of $~600Mn. Despite its size, it has a premium valuation relative to its peers. As of writing this, its FWD PE stood at ~44x. By comparison, larger semiconductor peers trade lower. For instance, Synopsys (SNPS) trades at ~32x and Rambus (RMBS) at 20x FWD (EPS). From a PEG perspective, CEVA is trading at ~2.3x as shown in Figure 4. This is quite high compared to the sector median at 1.54x, SNPS at 1.8x, and RMBS at 1.5x.

This high premium suggests that the market is expecting CEVA's higher growth rate and the long-term secular themes in which it participates. This is in the form of 20-30% licensing revenues and higher royalties in the future. And for sure, if you look at the growth grades, it is evident that the company has been growing faster than its sector median. From a revenue perspective, it's growing 187% faster than the sector median. On the other side, (EPS) is growing 42% faster than the sector median, as shown in Figure 5 below.

In absolute terms, the valuation is quite high. However, the FWD PEG of 2.3x may be justified if growth remains consistent and solid. By comparison, Synopsys has a lower top-line growth of ~13% and 20% EBITDA CAGR, and it's valued at ~30x FWD PE and 1.8x FWD PEG. I would say that the market is pricing high licensing and royalties ramps in the next 2–3 years. While the valuation is fair, if edge AI and IoT play out, then we should see the high multiples compressing to factor in the aggressive growth.

CEVA's Key Risks

The biggest risk I see for CEVA is competitive pressure. CEVA operates in an intensely competitive market. For edge AI, rivals include Arm's Ethos (ARM), Cadence Tensilica (CDNS), or custom ASIC solutions. When it comes to wireless IP, big players like Arm have Cortex, Dialog (ADI), and other foreign entrants competing on price and integration. Additionally, large OEMs like Apple (AAPL) and Google (GOOG) could develop in-house, and this could hurt CEVA's revenues significantly. Another risk is valuation risk. My thesis assumes that the management will execute its roadmap efficiently. However, if market assumptions do not hold, the stock could go down.

Final Thoughts

CEVA stands out as a leveraged play on the smart edge era, providing key building blocks for the next wave of intelligent devices. Its 1Q 2025 earnings results demonstrated continued momentum in licensing and significant design wins across Wi-Fi 6/7, Bluetooth, IoT, spatial audio, and edge AI. Even though royalty income lagged, the company's broad product roadmap for 5G and NPUs positions it to capture multiple growth vectors. Overall, CEVA is positioned for long-term upside if the industry adopts its IP at projected rates. Its diverse, high-value portfolio and strong engineering relationship suggest a sustained relevance. My view is that the company's strategic positioning in secular edge AI and connectivity markets makes it a great candidate for appreciation over a multi-year time period, assuming execution and growth stay on track.

data.title: K2 Alternative Strategies Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Global stocks declined during the quarter, despite a strong start, amid increasing worries about US trade policy and its impact on growth, as well as spending on artificial intelligence. Returns varied across regions, with the US trailing its developed market peers-boosted by strong gains in Europe-while a rally in China helped propel quarterly gains in broad emerging markets. Bonds broadly posted positive returns as yields declined, while commodities moved modestly higher.
  • Contributors: The relative value strategy drove the portfolio's modest quarterly advance, led by subadvisors Athena, Lazard and Apollo, all top overall contributors. From an asset class perspective, equity and cash positioning powered gains. The event driven strategy's slight advance was also accretive. Within the long-short equity strategy, subadvisor ActusRay was a leading overall contributor, powered by both the quantitative and discretionary portions of the subadvisor's portfolio.
  • Detractors: The long-short equity strategy weighed substantially on returns, as subadvisors Jennison and Electron were the largest overall detractors, owing to long equity positioning, especially in the health care sector-a focus for Jennison. At the portfolio level, long positioning in fixed income and short positioning in commodities helped drive losses. The Strategic Overlay hindered returns. The global macro strategy also detracted, due to subadvisor CFM, which started the year strong before commodity positioning-specifically shorts of copper and silver-helped create a quarterly loss.
  • Outlook: Uncertainty heightened in the first quarter, around economic growth, trade wars and geopolitical risks, weighing on stocks and helping widen credit spreads. We anticipate that continuing volatility is likely across market sand with it, dispersion. While we hold a neutral outlook toward most hedge fund strategies, we believe the coming months will present hedge fund managers with a favorable environment, because of elevated levels of dispersion. In our view, discretionary global macro and relative value strategies could be well-positioned to capitalize, along with volatility and risk-mitigation strategies.

Performance Review

  • After starting 2025 on a strong note, global equities collectively declined during the rest of the first quarter due to investor concerns about US economic growth, President Donald Trump's trade policy and a broadening trade war. Despite reaching new closing highs earlier in the quarter, the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) and the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) ended the quarter with negative returns. The technology-heavy NASDAQ Composite Index (COMP:IND) suffered a significantly larger decline as investors remained concerned about the potential effects of a Chinese company's new artificial intelligence model on US companies' Al spending. European stocks produced strong gains during the period amid signs of economic improvements and interest-rate cuts. Better-than-expected earnings reports by some companies and increased fiscal budgets by many of the region's countries, notably Germany, supported investor sentiment. In Asia, stocks collectively posted a modest gain, driven by the strong performance of Chinese stocks amid investor enthusiasm about the new Al model and signs of a more supportive stance from the Chinese government. As measured by MSCI indexes in US-dollar (USDOLLAR,DXY) terms, developed market equities modestly underperformed a global index, while emerging market equities significantly outpaced it with positive results. In terms of investment style, global value stocks generated strong gains and substantially outperformed global growth stocks. Most fixed income spread sectors recorded positive total returns during the quarter. Higher-rated credits generally fared better than their lower-rated counterparts. Over the period, US Treasury yields fell across much of the curve. With tailwind support from a depreciating US dollar and slight improvements in global manufacturing activity, the commodity complex's quarterly results skewed to the upside, though early strength gave way to bouts of weakness later the period. Commodity-linked energy and materials sector stocks broadly posted gains, both in the US and globally.
  • On a gross basis, two of four strategies contributed to the portfolio's performance in the first quarter, while six of the 10 underlying managers produced positive results. Gains were largely driven by the relative value strategy, as well as cash positioning. The event driven strategy was a slight contributor. Conversely, the long/short equity strategy and the Strategic Overlay weighed the most on returns. At the asset-class level, equity positioning, which was long overall, and cash positioning were the key contributors. In contrast, long fixed income positioning and commodity positioning (short overall) weighed the most on returns, with additional detraction from currency hedges.
  • The relative value strategy was the largest contributor to positive performance, due primarily to equity positioning, which was slightly long overall. The strategy was home three of the top four subadvisors, led by Athena, the quarter's largest overall contributor. Athena's equity and currency positioning, both long overall, drove the subadvisor's gains. Strength in industrials and communication services longs led the way in the equity portfolio. Long positions in the Japanese yen and the Norwegian krone bolstered currency gains, along with shorts of the US dollar. Lazard's strong performance was due to short equity positioning, partially offset by weakness in long fixed income positioning. All factors aided performance, with notable contribution from special situations. Subadvisor Apollo benefited from a conservatively positioned book and fixed income positioning, including a short position in a US-based pharmaceutical firm that traded down in sympathy with the market.
  • The event-driven strategy was a slight contributor as modest gains in long equity positioning were only partially offset by modest losses in currency positioning. Subadvisor Bardin Hill posted a modest gain, despite merger and acquisition volumes continuing to decline year over year. Top individual contributors included a Germany-based producer of polymer materials, one of Bardin Hill's largest positions, as spreads continued to tighten. A US-based commercial-stage biopharmaceutical company was a notable detractor, as the firm's acquisition timeline dragged amid pressure from market volatility late in the period.
  • The long/short equity was the quarter's largest detractor, weighed down by long equity positioning, especially in health care and equity index futures. Subadvisors Jennison and Electron were the quarter's largest detractors across all strategies, while ActusRay was a top overall contributor. Jennison's performance was primarily driven by long positions; the short book was positive as the market entered into a significant period of volatility and turbulence. Biotechnology stocks in the book were notable detractors, encompassing many of the largest detractors in Jennison's portfolio. Negative earnings reports weighed on some of these stocks, including shares of a diabetes care company. Electron, which had a strong January, was later impacted by the broader equity market sell-off as macroeconomic volatility increased, driven by the Trump administration's erratic tariff policy reversals and weaker consumer spending and sentiment. Detractors included a US-based electric utility whose shares declined along with the overall market despite a significant earnings beat. ActusRay was the strategy outlier, in a largely positive environment for quantitative investing. The discretionary piece of ActusRay's approach was also additive to returns. From a factor perspective, Value led the way.
  • The global macro strategy also weighed on returns, due largely to commodity (short overall) and long fixed income positioning. Currency positioning (short) also detracted meaningfully. At the subadvisor level, CFM was the lone detractor, despite a solid contribution in January. Three of six asset classes weighed on returns, with commodity positioning as the largest negative driver, owing to short positions in copper and silver. Long fixed income positioning also detracted. Subadvisor BlueBay was a modest contributor, while Graham posted a modest gain and had a limited impact. BlueBay's gains were mostly due to long fixed income positioning, especially in Columbia. Interest rate positioning also contributed.
  • The Strategic Overlay was a meaningful detractor, owing primarily to long positioning in NASDAQ 100 Index and S&P 500 Index futures.
  • During the quarter, the team increased exposure to the event driven strategy, while reducing exposure to the relative value strategy, through subadvisors Lazard and Athena.

Outlook

  • Entering 2025, we assessed that the investment climate was best characterized by high economic uncertainty, increased geopolitical risks, elevated valuations, and tight credit spreads. Fast forward to today, both economic uncertainty and geopolitical risks are even higher, valuations have begun to recede, and credit spreads are widening. We expect sustained market volatility and dispersion, fleeting liquidity during surprise news announcements, and crowded unwinds.
  • Given that the current market environment can still be characterized by high economic uncertainty, increased geopolitical risks, elevated valuations, and tight credit spreads, even with the hints of changing tides we noted above, we continue to believe the outlook for hedge fund strategies is very favorable. These factors are expected to lead to higher volatility across asset classes and increased dispersion within various sectors, particularly as political and economic influences impact sectors differently. This environment is particularly favorable for both relative value and active strategies that can be appreciated from being dynamic, non-directional, and agile.
  • We are optimistic about discretionary global macro strategies due to geopolitical uncertainty, changing interest rates, and shifts in foreign exchange rates. Relative value strategies appear promising, leveraging corporate events and asset class dispersion. Volatility and risk-mitigation strategies also offer diversification from traditional market risks.
  • We maintain a neutral rating for long/short equity, relative value, event driven, global macro and commodities strategies. Our outlook for relative value strategies is improving, due to higher political and economic risks creating increased opportunities for volatility and dispersion within and across asset classes. Relative value strategies have an advantage over directional investment styles at a time when the likely direction of markets is harder to predict. This challenge is particularly pronounced during inflection points. When there is a high degree of policy and economic uncertainty, there are strong tailwinds for some assets, and potentially excessive valuations for others. These uncertainties are beginning to be realized in the pricing of volatility across major asset classes. While volatility is generally considered a risk factor for directional strategies, it in fact presents better opportunities for managers that can capitalize on it through active trading in strategies such as volatility arbitrage, convertible bonds, and fixed income relative value
  • Within long/short equity, we prefer equity market neutral approaches. The early days of the Trump administration have not lived up to the optimistic consensus that followed the election results in November. Instead, trade and immigration policies are driving volatility higher. At the same time, the American consumer, which has continuously served as the foundation for a strong economy, might finally be feeling stretched. Given this setup, the beta outlook is cloudier than a few months ago. However, the alpha opportunity remains compelling. Volatility has the tendency to challenge returns for the strategy in the near term but can serve as an opportunity for managers to put capital to work at attractive entry points, thereby setting up for greater potential returns over the medium term.
  • We continue to favor forward-looking and tactical discretionary macro in an environment dominated by policy announcements that could have binary outcomes. As new policy is implemented and new market narratives emerge over the next several months, the opportunity set could shift toward longer-term thematic investors. Regional dispersion is increasingly in focus, especially with many managers finding opportunities to trade US vs. the rest of the world. Systematic strategies may continue to struggle if markets keep experiencing big shifts around news and policy announcements; however, we think the emergence of new trends and narratives around policy implementation could make the opportunity set more attractive over the medium term.
  • Turning to event driven, there remains a lot of pent-up demand for corporate deal-making activity, spanning activism, special situations, and M&A markets. However, that pace of activity has slowed meaningfully in recent months given uncertain policy messaging and increased economic uncertainty. Management team confidence can be a real drag on deal-making activity, which has been observed so far this year. And, lower deal volumes mean a more competitive and less attractively priced merger arbitrage environment, with more efficient pricing and fewer trading opportunities. There are meaningful potential tailwinds to these strategies should the uncertainty abate, so we remain optimistic for the strategy in the long term. In the intermediate stages, however, the elevated volatility and higher uncertainty makes for a more challenging environment, which favors more defensive and highly nimble market participants.
  • In the commodities space, relative value strategies may be well-positioned to benefit from recent market dislocations, including those brought about by trade policy expectations and implementation. Directional strategies may continue to be challenged by range-bound markets, especially as markets frequently reprice the potential for opposing outcomes around major geopolitical events (e.g., a Russia/Ukraine peace deal). Long-term themes like the energy transition remain intact but could continue to experience higher volatility around macro headlines.

data.title: Gold Fields: Complete Takeover Of Gruyere Strengthens Prospects
data.description:
A “Hold” Rating for Gold Fields Limited

This analysis recommends a “Hold” rating for NYSE-listed shares of Gold Fields Limited (NYSE:GFI), which is consistent with our previous article.

This company, as we wrote in our previous article, is:

“a South African gold producer that has assets not only onshore but also in South America (Chile and Peru), Ghana, Australia and Canada.”

This recommendation assumes that investors continue to hold shares of the company, hereinafter referred to as "Gold Fields" or "GFI," as we believe the expected positive gold price outlook aligns well with GFI's business prospects. Hold until the share price declines. At that point, investors may even want to increase their positions. We are impressed by GFI's investment vision for long-term business growth, which will strengthen over time given the company's improving and growing portfolio of profitable mines and strong balance sheet.

Stock Performance Since Our Previous Analysis

Since our last “Hold” recommendation on November 6, 2024, Gold Fields' NYSE-listed shares have performed well. The expected drivers materialised, delivering shareholders a gain of +40.92% and a total return of +43.52%, including the +2.6% dividend yield. The stock thus outperformed the overall US equity market, as the benchmark S&P 500 index recorded a negative change of -5.31% at the time of writing.

The driving force was “a stronger H2 performance”, as Seeking Alpha reported on February 20, 2025, highlighting the crucial support provided by the rising gold price. The uptrend pushed the price per ounce up 25% year-on-year to $2,418/ounce in 2024, amid geopolitical tensions and macroeconomic headwinds that fueled strong demand for safe-haven assets.

This significantly offset the lower and more expensive production activities and led to an astonishing increase in profits for the South African mining company.

Seeking Alpha wrote:

“The South African miner said net profit jumped to $1.24B last year from $703.3M in 2023, supported by rising gold prices, even though its gold production fell 10% Y/Y to 2.07M oz, while all-in sustaining costs for 2024 increased 26% to $1,629/oz; in November, the company said it expected full-year production at the lower end of its previous 2.05M-2.15M oz guidance range.”

Other profitability and cash flow indicators that can usually attract market interest include the following, which must have largely played their role:

  • The 17.3% year-on-year jump in adjusted EBITDA to $2,847.4 million for the full year 2024 provides ample assurance that Gold Fields is a safe haven for capital, while providing ample guarantees of repayment, even after a considerable debt increase (from $1,672.9 million in 2023 to $2,945.8 million in 2024), to support growth, particularly through acquisitions. This is reflected in a net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio of 0.73, suggesting that Gold Fields, if required by lenders, could repay its debt capital in just under 12 months. And far from such a scenario, which certainly does not suit GFI, as we are talking about a globally recognized producer of the yellow metal that is now rising sharply in the markets, Wall Street will be happy to remind you that investors generally do not require more than 4-5 for this financial leverage ratio, or as low as 3 for the most conservative. In this situation, lenders should not rule out providing the South African gold mining company with additional capital, if necessary, although due to inflation fears triggered by the tariff war, lending remains severely constrained globally.
  • The 26% jump in operating cash inflow (“OCF”) to US$1,986 million in 2024, driven by higher gold prices and the release of working capital, proved to be ample coverage despite the overwhelming capital expenditure (“CapEx”) item rising by a significant 12% year-on-year to US$1,183 million in 2024 among the needs of operating activities. Since market judgments are heavily influenced by a company's return to its key stakeholders, shareholders and lenders, GFI's excess cash flow from operations is a welcome development for the market. And especially now, when the economic cycle is challenging for companies, it takes on even greater specific weight. Investors may want to consider the positive implications for GFI's US-listed shares.
  • Free cash flow (“FCF”) – roughly measured as OCF less CapEx, which is US$774.5 million, or more accurately as “adjusted FCF,” which is US$605.1 million – improved by an impressive 53% and 65%, respectively, in 2024 compared to the previous year. Investors and lenders are usually not indifferent to this kind of progress, as it enables the return of value to stakeholders who have the biggest voice in the stock market. The allocation to dividend payments, also seen as the non-retained portion of earnings that drives the sentiment in the US stock market, was $379 million in 2024 for Gold Fields shareholders, on par with the previous year.

A future share repurchase program cannot be ruled out either as GFI keeps on generating robust free cash flow, according to key operating and financial performance information for the company reported by Seeking Alpha:

“Gold Fields (GFI) raised its dividend by 34% to a company record 10 rand/share ($0.54) per share, prompting CEO Mike Fraser to say the company may consider a share buyback program as a way to boost shareholder returns if the gold price remains elevated.”

The good result of free cash flow enabled the repayment of loans (of ≈$986 million in 2024, an increase of 1.5 times compared to the previous year), which helped to strengthen the confidence of lenders in GFI's overall situation. Aided by the astonishing rise in the gold price in 2024, the excess of debt inflows over loan repayments was ten times higher than in 2023, giving Gold Fields a total cash inflow of US$1,213 million in 2024 (a 15-fold increase compared to the previous year), bringing the total loan drawdowns to $2,291 million as of end of 2024. The loan generates interest expenses that GFI is able to cover, as evidenced by the TTM Interest Coverage Ratio of 79.4x (= Operating Income of US$ 1,984.8 million for the 12 months ending December 30, 2024 / Interest Expenses of US$ 25 million for the 12 months ending December 30, 2024), as investors start from a minimum level of 1.5 to 2.0x.

In addition, “Total cash and ST investments” were $860.2 million as of December 30, 2024, compared to $648.7 million as of December 30, 2023.

These earnings metrics and Gold Fields' improved credit profile in the eyes of lenders were supported by a 16% year-on-year revenue growth to $5,202 million in 2024, driven by a 25% higher gold price, which more than offset the 10% year-on-year decline in gold equivalent ounces sold to 2.151 million ounces. This was primarily due to lower production in the first half of 2024, but the trend, as we signalled in the previous article, actually turned positive again in the second half of 2024.

We wrote:

“With the backfill issues at South Deep in South Africa, the Gruyere mine production setback in Australia, and the Salares Norte ramp-up in northern Chile on recovery mode, Gold Fields expects things to improve a lot starting in H2-2024 targeting 1.11 million oz to 1.21 million oz at AISC of US$1,460/oz to US$1,580/oz.”

As South Deep resolved issues with backfill leakage and rehandling issues, and the completion of open pits helped St Ives Gold Mine in Western Australia's mining operations, the situation changed completely for the better, as the company stated:

“There was a significant turnaround during the second half of 2024, with zero fatalities and a material step-up in our operating performance. Second half production was 26% higher than in H1 2024 and our AISC decreased by 12%, allowing the Group to meet the revised full-year guidance for both production and costs.”

Outlook (2025): Gold Production, Costs and Gold Prices

Production and Costs:

“For 2025, attributable gold equivalent production is expected to be between 2.250Moz - 2.450Moz (compared to 2.071Moz delivered in 2024). AISC is expected to be between US$1,500/oz - US$1,650/oz, [...] according to Gold Fields Limited in its " Reviewed Results for the Year Ended December 31, 2024." ”

The AISC for 2024 went up by 26% year-on-year to $1,629/ounce. Thus, AISC will be on average 3.3% lower in 2025 than in 2024.

Gold Price Forecasts:

According to analysts at major US banks, the goal of seeking a safe haven amid geopolitical tensions and macroeconomic problems will also strengthen the yellow metal in the coming period, so they have issued the following gold price forecasts:

Goldman Sachs:

Goldman Sachs Group Inc. now expects the price of “the metal could hit $4,000 an ounce midway through next year.”, according to Yahoo Finance.

About the other US bank giant, JPMorgan, Yahoo Finance wrote:

“The bank now expects gold prices to reach an average of $3,675/oz by 4Q25, on the way towards above $4,000/oz by 2Q26, with risks skewed towards an earlier overshoot of these forecasts if demand surpasses its expectations.”

ANZ:

“investors eyeing more gains in the coming days.”, as Seeking Alpha wrote around mid-April, adding the following statement from ANZ:

"We believe risk-off purchases for gold are yet to pick up," ANZ said, as it increased its year-end gold price forecast to $3,600 per ounce and six-month forecast to $3,500.

Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR) was $3,308.06/ounce at the time of writing, up +26% year-to-date or +43.3% over the past 12 months.

With rising gold prices and production, as well as expected, lower costs, the profit margin will be such that shareholders can be optimistic about their investments in GFI, as the positive market sentiment will continue to welcome dividends and a stronger financial position to support these growth projects.

Growth Projects

The proposed joint venture with AngloGold Ashanti (AU) to combine Gold Fields' Tarkwa operation with neighbouring AngloGold Ashanti's Iduapriem gold mine in Ghana must be considered on hold for now because Seeking Alpha reports:

“The pause will allow them to focus on maximizing performance at their respective sites independently, while AngloGold Ashanti works to consolidate improvements in its long-term mining plan — currently considered its most valuable option.”

Gold Fields has acquired Gold Road Resources (OTCPK:ELKMF) in a$2.4 billion deal, becoming a 100% owner of “the low-cost, long-life Gruyere gold mine in Western Australia”, reported Seeking Alpha.

Pending the completion of the transaction, GFI and Gold Road hold a 50:50 interest in the mine through their existing joint venture.

The transaction can be financed by leveraging GFI's credit profile described above and is supported by favorable gold price, cost, and production forecasts. Assuming GFI were to increase its bank exposure to finance the acquisition of the Gruyère Gold Mine, net debt would increase to $4.5 billion. To stay within the 0.7x net debt to adjusted EBITDA threshold, the latter component of the ratio would need to be approximately $6.4 billion. If a gold margin of $800/ounce (= $2,418/ounce equivalent sold in 2024 less an AISC of $1,619/ounce) results in adjusted EBITDA of $2,847.4 million or $1,323.75/ounce (/2.151 million ounces) in 2024, then adjusted EBITDA for 2025, with a projected gold margin of $1,800/ounce (= expected gold price of $3,400 less an AISC of $1,600/ounce), is well on track to be at a level that matches the leverage ratio. This is without taking into account the effects of increasing liquidity due to a rising gold price margin: if a 4.6% year-on-year increase in gold margin led to a 32.6% year-on-year increase to $860.2 million in cash on hand, with a margin that could potentially increase by 125%, GFI would end up with an avalanche of liquidity available in 2025. This would result in significantly lower net debt than the estimated $4.5 billion and make the work on adjusted EBITDA much easier, as the positive implications from higher production/higher gold prices/lower costs would be passed on to net debt through increased liquidity and to adjusted EBITDA that would be higher as well.

In short, the record uptrend in the gold price allows Gold Fields to fully own the production at the Gruyere gold mine, which “is expected to produce 325K-350K oz of gold this year.” as Seeking Alpha says, following 287.3 thousand ounces produced in 2024 at an AISC of US$1,619/ounce.

“ Gold Fields (NYSE: GFI) also said it plans to start building a new mine at its Windfall project in Quebec this year, with production targeted to begin in 2028.”, wrote Seeking Alpha.

Gold Fields is the sole owner of the Windfall gold project in Quebec after GFI acquired the second 50% interest from Osisko Mining for CA$2 billion in net cash. According to Osisko's December 2022 feasibility study, Windfall is expected to produce≈ 300,000 ounces of gold per year. Production will span a mine life of≈ 10 years and, at an AISC of $758 per ounce, will have quite a mitigating effect on GFI's cost structure. The facilities have historically received consistent investments of over CA$1 billion through the GFI-Osisko Mining joint venture, but ongoing activities and some permitting are still pending. Therefore, Gold Fields does not expect the first gold shipment from Windfall until late in the fourth quarter of 2026.

About expenditure plans, the company says

“Sustaining capital is expected to be US$940m – US$970m [...] driven largely by capital waste stripping at Gruyere and Tarkwa, as well as underground development at Granny Smith. Non-sustaining capex is expected to be US$550m – US$580m, with the largest component of this being the Windfall Project capital of US$400m and the St Ives renewable power project of US$110m.”

The above is compared to sustained capital expenditures of $849 million and nonsustained capital expenditures of $334 million in 2024. However, Gold Fields has an investment-grade credit rating, which the company can use to its advantage to raise more financing should it be necessary. And most importantly, the bullish gold price is its strongest ally.

Stock Price: Provided The Prospects Are Robust, Seeking the Dips is Always Preferable

NYSE-listed shares of Gold Fields Limited were trading at $22.21 per share at the time of writing, representing a market cap of $20.55 billion, having risen 38% in the past year. GFI offers a dividend yield (“TTM”) of $0.56, which translates to a yield (“TTM”) of 2.42%.

The stock is not currently trading low, based on its comparison to MA Ribbon, and also because it is above the midpoint of $19.25 per share within its 52-week range of $12.98 to $25.52 per share. Also, the 14-day Relative Strength Indicator of 49.67 suggests that there is a lot of room for shares to fall back to healthier lower levels. Investors can use these levels to strengthen their positions with an eye on the upcoming future that promises to be exciting.

It’s worth waiting for a more attractive entry point, as gold prices are in a bit of a trough (2.5% drop from the previous session at the time of writing) after the Federal Reserve took a wait-and-see approach on Wednesday. After reaffirming rates at 4.25-4.50% for the third time in a row, officials agreed that there was no point in cutting rates too quickly. Therefore, no action will be taken until it is clear whether the tariffs will take effect and what the impact will be on inflation, the economy, and employment. The Fed's wait-and-see stance will boost interest in the bond market but create headwinds for gold, as the market may lose a little bit of appetite for the precious metal as a zero-yield security. The NYSE shares of South African gold producer GFI are positively correlated with the price of the yellow metal and are therefore also subject to a slightly depressed mood, albeit temporarily.

Conclusion

Gold Fields Limited is a South African gold producer with assets not only on the South African mainland, but also in South America, Ghana, Australia, and Canada. Thanks to the record uptrend in the gold price, GFI's earnings and free cash flow have performed remarkably well. The company has improved its financial position and now has an even better investment-grade rating, which benefits its portfolio's focus on robust growth, including through acquisitions.

The company recently made significant investments to acquire the second 50% of the Gruyère gold mine in Western Australia, which it did not previously own. Based on available information, this mine promises to be a long-life, low-cost asset that will contribute significantly to GFI's profitability. GFI is well-positioned given increasing production, cooling costs, and soaring gold prices. Earnings are the primary driver of sentiment in the U.S. equity market. A "Hold" recommendation means maintaining positions to benefit from a promising future and buying shares once the share price returns to lower levels. The Fed's wait-and-see interest rate policy could favor a downward trend in share prices.
