data.title: My 5 Biggest Dividend Investments I Trust With Half Of My Net Worth
data.description:
Introduction

So far this year, I have deployed way more capital than I initially expected, as the market offered a lot of opportunities. I invested in energy stocks, cyclical industrials, and I even added a new stock to my portfolio. I also consolidated two of my railroad holdings.

In general, during the market's most volatile days last month, I was one of the market's biggest bulls, writing articles with titles like "My Worst Week Ever - And The 4 Dividend Stocks I'm Buying Now."

With that said, the market has recovered substantially since then.

However, I am by no means writing this article to take a victory lap, as I never claimed to know that the market would bottom. To me, it is always about the risk/reward. When I like it, I buy, as I believe a solid long-term investment strategy does not even require perfect timing.

One of the charts I used last month is the one below, which shows that whenever we encounter a massive short-term volatility spike, it's usually a good time to deploy some capital.

I believe one of the biggest mistakes investors make is waiting for all the bad news to 'go away' during downtrends, which often keeps them from buying fantastic companies at great valuations.

During strong bull markets, it may feel much safer to invest, yet the risk/reward isn't always better.

Bad news often creates an environment of fear, as the focus is on potentially unfavorable developments. During bull markets, we also deal with risks, except that they have not materialized yet. It could be the perception of safety, if that makes sense.

In this case, fear was (and still is) based on trade uncertainty, as the Bloomberg Economics Global Trade Uncertainty index shows. Given how interconnected the world has become, an escalating trade war could do serious economic (and market) damage.

When adding that the S&P 500 wasn't cheap to begin with, going into this year, it makes sense that investors and traders get nervous.

It also means that we are not out of the woods yet.

But then again, to me, it's all about the risk/reward of the market and single stocks when these fears cause stock prices to go south.

Anyway, there are multiple reasons why I am writing this article.

  • Because of aggressive investing, the top five of my holdings have changed quite a bit in recent months/quarters.
  • My five biggest investments account for roughly half of my portfolio. As my portfolio accounts for nearly my entire net worth, these stocks are crucial for my investment goals.
  • Also, and I'm being brutally honest here, because I am currently traveling, it takes me multiple days to write an article, which means writing an article that is not macro-heavy (headline-sensitive) makes my life a lot easier.

That said, I'm not cutting corners here, as discussing my biggest investments is one of the most serious things I will be doing this month, as there's a lot more to it than simply listing them.

And, on a side note, I am really sorry that all of my largest holdings have very low yields. While they are dividend growers, they are unlikely to be the right choice for income-focused investors. So, my apologies for that.

Nonetheless, I will write this article in such a way that it still provides info for the people who are unlikely to ever buy these stocks.

So, as we have a lot to discuss, let's get to it!

#1 & #2 - The Permian Land Duo Of Texas Pacific Land Corp (TPL) and LandBridge (LB)

I know what you're thinking right now.

Probably something along the lines of "He just told us he isn't cutting corners, and now he's combining two picks."

That's because these picks are based on the same idea and because these two companies are very similar. LandBridge is often called a "mini-TPL."

These two companies account for roughly 30% of my entire portfolio, which shows just how much confidence I have in these stocks. Also, a reason behind this large exposure is their terrific performance, as both stocks have done really well since I bought them.

What makes these stocks so special is their business model.

Texas Pacific, which is slightly larger in my portfolio than LandBridge, owns more than 870 thousand surface acres in the Permian Basin, America's most important oil and gas basin. It got these assets from the Texas & Pacific Railway bankruptcy in the 19th century.

LandBridge owns more than 270 thousand surface acres. Most of these came from aggressive M&A, as the company was founded just a few years ago by Five Point Energy, an energy holding company that owns diversified energy assets like LandBridge and WaterBridge (not public).

What makes these business models so strong is their simplicity.

TPL and LB are the only public companies in the United States that own surface, water, and mineral rights, meaning they make money on everything that happens on and under their land.

This includes royalties for oil and gas (roughly 50% of TPL revenues and 8% of LB revenues) and royalties/revenues related to water and commercial operations.

See, I am almost more interested in the water business than oil and gas, as fracking operations require a lot of water to pump chemicals into the ground. This process also produces a lot of produced (toxic) water, which oil companies need to get rid of.

As the data below shows, the water business has been booming for TPL. Because of declining oil and gas reserve quality, more water is needed for production, and more produced water needs to be disposed of.

LandBridge was initially founded to manage the land of WaterBridge, which is one of the biggest water infrastructure companies in the nation. LandBridge makes money from the pipelines that are on its land and generates royalties from the volumes that flow through these pipes.

Moreover, LandBridge has a large focus on commercial operations, based on the "powered land" concept, where is offers land with dirt-cheap energy, 'unlimited' cooling water, great power line access, and so much more to operators of data centers, hydrogen plants, and everything requiring a lot of energy and remote land.

This makes LB an indirect AI player, as I believe the Permian is perfect for data center developments, especially as large cities are reaching their limits due to the massive power and water demand of these mega-scale data centers.

Texas Pacific also aims for these operations. However, unlike LB, its land is mostly non-contiguous, which makes it harder to bring data centers on board without acquiring more land.

Moreover, because neither TPL nor LB needs to invest a substantial amount of capital to keep the business going, as that's almost entirely up to its tenants/customers, they have ultra-high margins.

As we can see below, LB (the blue circle) and TPL (the purple circle) have adjusted EBITDA margins of roughly 90% and free cash flow margins of more than 60%, meaning 60 cents of every revenue dollar turns into free cash flow.

Also, Texas Pacific Land has no gross debt.

While both have yields of less than 1% (I will not and cannot sugarcoat that), they have the perfect business models for long-term production in the Permian, booming water demand, and an acceleration of commercial developments.

Also, because of their high margins, both are in a great spot to protect investors against inflation and even very low oil and gas prices, as both companies collect royalties instead of spending capital on production.

Having said all of this, current oil price dynamics make it very likely that Permian production will come down, which will likely hurt royalty volumes and prices. It will also hurt water demand growth.

However, as I wrote in a recent article, I believe what OPEC is doing is bullish for the long term, as it is pressuring 'cheaters' to fall back in line and stick to OPEC production targets. When adding maturing oil production in every single major non-Permian basin in the United States, I am very bullish on the oil part of TPL's and LB's businesses.

As a result, I continue to add to TPL and LB on dips. While I am sometimes annoyed by their volatility (both had >14% daily declines in April), I cannot think of a duo I would rather own for the decades that lie ahead.

#3 - Comfort Systems USA (FIX)

Until this year, I had never heard of Comfort Systems USA. Now, it's my third-largest investment...

Essentially, both LandBridge and Comfort Systems are companies I bought just days after I had found out about them, despite that most of my investments spent months (if not years) on my watchlist before I bought them.

LandBridge went public last year. When I found out what the company did, I spent many days nonstop researching the company.

I did the same with Comfort Systems, as I found the stock earlier this year when I looked for businesses with very high returns on invested capital, elevated dividend growth, and highly unique business models.

Once I found it, I spent my nights doing in-depth research.

Now, it's my third-largest investment, because a substantial amount of my large cash position went into FIX. It also helped that I bought a lot more last month during the crash. Now, I'm up 23%.

With that said, Comfort Systems is what I like to call a very large plumbing company, as it owns a decentralized network of HVAC companies that focus on areas like ventilation, plumbing, piping, off-site construction, fire protection, and more.

This business portfolio is the result of countless M&A deals that have given he company one major advantage, which is its size.

Because of its network, it can take on much larger contracts. This includes specialized work for stadiums, airports, pharmaceutical facilities, and data centers, among others.

As we can see below, last year, it generated the majority of its revenue from technology and manufacturing customers, as the data center boom and economic re-shoring trends have created one of the strongest bull cases imaginable. Yes, really.

Hence, despite cyclical economic weakness, the company is hitting it out of the park. In 1Q25, it saw $800 million sequential backlog growth, pushing the total backlog to nearly $7 billion, a number nearly five times larger than before 2021.

With regard to shareholder distributions, the company yields 0.4%.

This low yield isn't the company's fault, as the five-year CAGR is 27.2%. On April 24, it hiked its dividend by 12.5%. The massive stock price surge of the past ten years has more than offset dividend growth. That's good news for shareholders who bought in the past but bad news for investors looking to buy income.

Moreover, the company's payout ratio is just 8%, as the focus remains on M&A. While I expect the company to eventually pay a 'decent' yield (a 40% payout ratio implies a 2.0% yield, it spends roughly 75% of its cash on M&A, a strategy that continues to pay off handsomely.

In light of aggressive M&A, it helps that the company has a superior return on invested capital ratio, which shows it's extremely talented when it comes to putting capital to work.

This is what I wrote in my latest in-depth article on its high ROIC:

Reasons behind this performance include the company's focus on high-demand areas like data centers and semiconductor plants. These operations come with premium pricing, as it's not a random plumbing company.

Moreover, MEP contracting requires relatively low fixed capital investment compared to manufacturing or heavy construction. Most capital is tied to working capital (e.g., receivables, inventory), not large plants or equipment.

Valuation-wise, we're dealing with a company expected to show cumulative EPS growth of more than 50% in 2025-2027. Applying its 10-year average P/E ratio of 22x to these numbers, we get a base-case fair stock price target of $490, 13% above the current price.

Personally, I expect the stock price to rise far beyond that number, as the company basically has no support from cyclical growth at all right now.

Once that factor is added to the mix, I think we're dealing with one of the strongest compounders in the industrial space.

Speaking of industrial gems, the next one is also a standout, I bought a lot more of this year.

#4 - Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL)

When it comes to efficiently putting capital to work, Old Dominion is a standout as well, as it has an ROIC of 27%, a number that has put the stock on the radar of many dividend growth-focused investors.

Old Dominion is a company I had on my watchlist for years before I finally pulled the trigger roughly one year ago.

Although I always wanted to avoid trucks, I bought Old Dominion Freight Line because of its dominant position in the less-than-truckload business. Unlike truckload businesses, LTL players rely on a dense network of distribution centers, which allows them to efficiently pick up and distribute goods for a wide range of clients, most of whom are industrial clients.

Old Dominion operates 261 service centers. Almost all of them are owned by the company, which saves a lot of money by not paying rent and owning top-tier industrial assets close to major demand markets all over the United States.

The company's network has made it the most efficient LTL provider, which has won the Mastio Quality Award for 15 straight years. The data from Mastio also shows that the company has, by far, the best offering, which provides it with superior pricing power. This, too, is a huge factor.

It is also the only company with a (narrow) moat in its industry.

Thanks to these benefits:

  • ODFL has grown its market share from 2.9% in 2002 to 12% in 2023.
  • It has grown its revenue by 10.3% per year from 2004-2024.
  • It has the best operating ratio in the industry, meaning it is (by far) the most efficient player.

It also has more cash than gross debt, which gives it the healthiest balance sheet of the entire industry.

With regard to its dividend, the company yields 0.7%. This dividend has a 20% payout ratio and a five-year CAGR of 34%, one of the highest numbers on the entire stock market.

Although the freight market is currently struggling, I happily use weakness to add to my position, which is why I aggressively bought more in April. That's why it's now a top 5 position.

Going forward, analysts expect a 6% EPS contraction in 2025, which makes sense in light of macroeconomic headwinds. 2026 and 2027 are expected to see 17% and 14% growth, respectively.

If we get a growth bottom, I expect these numbers to improve significantly.

Now comes a stock with a somewhat decent yield.

It's a stock I have discussed countless times in the past. It's also one of the first holdings.

#5 - Union Pacific Corp. (UNP)

Union Pacific was one of the first five dividend stocks I bought in 2020. I didn't start my career back then, but I decided it was time for me to get serious about long-term dividend investing.

Union Pacific has always been one of my largest investments, as I am a big railroad fan. That's not because trains are 'cool' but because North American Class I railroads enjoy wide business moats.

In our view, each of the North American Class I railroads we cover, including Union Pacific, enjoys a wide economic moat rooted in cost advantages and efficient scale. Core pricing and margin resilience in past freight recessions and in the face of substantial coal volume losses over the past decade-plus are a testament to their robust competitive positioning. With near certainty, we expect the rails to continue to turn their two core moat sources into economic profit for the next 10 years, and more likely than not 20 years from now. - Morningstar (emphasis added)

In April, I sold Norfolk Southern (NSC) and put the proceeds into Union Pacific. That move was based on consolidating my railroad investments, not because I started to dislike NSC. I just preferred Union Pacific's bulk/merchandise-focused business model.

Generally speaking, I like Union Pacific's footprint a lot, as it has a duopoly with Buffett-owned BNSF in the Western two-thirds of the United States. This includes access to Canada and Mexico through partnerships and its 26% stake in Ferromex, which is Mexico's leading railroad.

With that said, I often get asked by people why I have bought so much (and so frequently) Union Pacific in recent years, despite the fact that its stock price hasn't gone anywhere.

Like all of its peers, Union Pacific is cyclical, which means investors usually don't get excited until growth expectations improve.

The chart below compares the Union Pacific stock price (% below its all-time high) to the leading ISM Manufacturing Index (black line). Lower growth expectations are a drag on the stock, which explains why UNP hasn't gone anywhere since early 2022. This makes it a three-year sideways trend.

The reason I'm buying so much is that I want to use these periods to accumulate UNP shares. Once we enter another cyclical growth upswing, I want to be prepared. When that happens, I will shift my focus to other undervalued areas.

That's how I usually work, as I put capital to work in areas that don't get the love they deserve. Once they do, I move over to other sectors and industries.

Needless to say, Union Pacific isn't just a random cyclical stock. On top of having a wide moat, it is also the most efficient North American Class I railroad, as it has lowered its operating ratio (inverse operating margin) to 60.7% in 1Q25, a phenomenal number in light of inflation and demand challenges.

It also affirmed its 2025 outlook, which is good news in light of elevated macroeconomic challenges.

As a result, the company, which has an A-rated balance sheet, boosted buybacks this year, supported by an 80% cash flow conversion rate.

While the five-year dividend CAGR has come down to 7.0% due to economic challenges since 2021, the company still has one of the best dividend track records, having grown its dividend for nearly 20 consecutive years.

Over the past ten years alone, the dividend has been hiked by 144%. During this period, the company has also bought back nearly a third of its shares.

With regard to its valuation, analysts expect a recovery to double-digit EPS growth in both 2026 and 2027. When adding that UNP trades at 19.0x earnings, below its longer-term average of 21.3x, we get a theoretical total return outlook of nearly 16%.

All things considered, I am extremely happy Union Pacific is one of my largest holdings, as there are not many companies I trust to deliver substantial returns for many decades to come.

I trust it so much that I am not at all bothered by its cyclical behavior.

Takeaway

I didn't write this article to celebrate what one might call a lucky bounce in the market.

I wrote it to highlight my investment process, the same one I follow regardless of headlines and market sentiment.

Although it sounds much easier than it is, I invest when the risk/reward looks attractive, not when it feels safe.

That mindset led me to aggressively add to my portfolio earlier this year, including expanding my exposure to two of my highest-conviction plays, which are Texas Pacific Land and LandBridge.

I also introduced Comfort Systems USA as a new top-three position after intense research, added a lot to Old Dominion, and nearly doubled my Union Pacific investment through consolidation.

These stocks may not fit everyone's income needs, but their long-term compounding potential is hard to ignore.

Essentially, volatility will always be part of the deal, but I'm not interested in perfect timing. All I care about is owning world-class businesses with durable advantages.

When I find them, I tend to go a little overboard when it comes to deploying capital. So far, it's a strategy that has worked, and I am very upbeat about the durability of this approach.

data.title: Coherent: Too Cheap To Ignore
data.description:
My thesis

Coherent (NYSE:COHR) is the world's largest optical, laser, and photonics company and has been delivering strong results in its most significant segment, the data center vertical, for a couple of quarters. However, this is unappreciated by investors, pricing the stock as one of the cheapest in its peer group, with a FW 2027 P/E of 13X. The stock has declined in correlation with the tech correction, but just published Q3 results show underlying growth strength and a solid margin expansion despite tariff impacts. My DCF indicates a significant upside potential with conservative estimates. I keep my BUY rating on the stock.

Takeaway from recent conference calls

Before navigating through just-published Q3 results, let's quickly look at Q2 result highlights, as a quick reminder. Published in February, Q2FY25 has beaten sales expectations by +4.8%, printing a new record quarterly revenue at $1.43bn (+27% YoY and +6% QoQ). Group results were driven by a continuing growth in the Datacom segment, raising +79% YoY, and a recovery in the Telecom vertical (+11% YoY). However, Industrials and other markets have shown signs of stabilization. In the data center segment, Coherent is benefiting from the strength of 400Gps and 800Gps data transceivers and will ramp up in S2 FY25 its latest generation with 1.6Tbytes speeds. The management said:

We continue to see expansion in the number of customers adopting and ramping our 800 gig transceivers. After delivering initial samples of our 1.6T datacom transceivers to customers last year, we remain on track to begin ramping sales in calendar 2025.. Even as we focus on the 1.6T ramp, we are investing and innovating for the future. We are both developing our 3.2T transceivers.

The transition from 400 Gbps to higher data speeds is expected to be gradual, as shown in the following graph from Dell'Oro.

This transceiver growth is not without risks. Recent articles indicate that the threat of co-packaged optics, called CPOs, where optics are integrated directly into the networking switch, could negatively impact parts of Coherent's business. The following illustrative picture from Broadcom (AVGO) shows a consequence of such a transition.

Let's now turn to just the Q3 result. Revenues reached $1.5bn, a new record, growing +24% YoY and +5% QoQ. The data center segment drove the trend, representing 60% of total sales and growing by +46% YoY. On the contrary, industrial activities were nearly flat YoY, a similar trend to the previous quarter.

We can see in the following graph that Communication (mainly data centers) has been the core driver of the growth over the quarters. It is therefore essential that Cloud and Hyperscaler CAPEX are not slowing down. Comments on materials for the new laser generation for data centers were bullish:

To meet rising demand for optical networking solutions that use either EML or CW lasers, we continue to expand our indium phosphide capacity. In Q3 we once again expanded our capacity both sequentially and year-over-year with the year-over-year capacity growing by over 3x.

In the end, the CEO expressed his confidence in FY25 trends: "We continue to expect fiscal 2025 to be a strong growth year for the company, and we believe we are well positioned for continued long-term growth." It added that more details will be given during the next Investor Day, on the 28th of May.

Coherent is not just a topline story; a big part of the stock rerating should be unlocked through margin expansions. In Q3, gross margins expanded by 490 basis points YoY, though higher average selling prices in the data centers, pricing optimization, and closure of less profitable segments (such as devices and modules for the SiC business). EBIT margins jumped by 608bps YoY due to OPEX leverage and cost-cutting programs in place. Management guidance for the next quarter indicates non-GAAP gross margins between 37% and 39%, which is resilient and indicates little tariff-related impact.

The management said: "We also continued to make solid progress towards achieving our gross margin target of operating above 40% on a non-GAAP basis," and added: "The current environment related to tariffs is not significant."

Coherent is present worldwide and can count on more than 60 facilities: it is well diversified geographically.

What valuation can we expect?

I revised my mid-term hypotheses by normalizing growth downward, as client data center CAPEX trends remain uncertain in FY26 after a spending boom in FY25.

  • Management comments on key financial metrics concerning FY25.
  • Hereafter, I modeled a 7% CAGR growth trend by the addressable market trends highlighted by the company.
  • Gross margin expansion due to a growing mix toward more profitable data center products.
  • Operating leverage, with OPEX growing 10% in the next three years, then +5%/year as the company matures.

With a terminal growth of 3% and a WACC of 9%, I get a target price of $104/share, indicating a 50% upside potential.

Risks

The main risk relates to competition. While being number one or two in its end markets with a market share close to 20%, it is still facing a couple of competitors, as can be seen below. The biggest ones are the Chinese firm Innolight, Lumentum (LITE), Broadcom, and Marvell (MRVL). In the SiC segment, it is also facing Wolfspeed (WOLF) and Chinese competitors.

The second most significant risk comes from potential growth disappointment caused by data center trends, especially in FY26, as Cloud and IA tech companies have not yet laid out their CAPEX plans. The following networking data center peer group has suffered losses YTD and is trying to recover from its lows.

A supportive aspect could come from its relatively cheap valuation compared to its peers. As seen below, forward P/E ratios are low. Not many expectations are priced into the stock, so there must be a margin of safety for unexpected disappointment.

Conclusion

Coherence is delivering strong results in the data center segment without deserving the correct valuation. Fundamentals are improving with margin expansion, increasing cash flow generation, and debt deleveraging. The share dip represents a buying opportunity. I keep my buy rating.
